mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reconditioned units run £2,500–£14,000 supply only depending on generation and engine [EM-QUOTE] — from a Classic Tdi at £2,500–£4,500 to the L460's P530 V8 at £9,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Range Rover engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.4 SDV8 (448DT) — the lowest failure-enquiry rate of any big JLR engine in our data despite the highest replacement cost. From the BMW era, the M57 Td6 deserves the same reputation.

mainEntity.name: Which Range Rover engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT) without documented oil-interval history — its crankshaft-bearing failure leaves nothing to rebuild. The 3.6 TDV8 (368DT) needs its timing-chain history evidenced. Unrebuild P38 V8s carry liner risk priced into every listing.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Range Rover engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: By engine: cold-start knock (306DT), timing chain rattle (368DT, AJ133), supercharger whine (5.0 SC), coolant loss and overheating (P38 V8). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Entirely generation-dependent. L405/L460: almost always ✅. L322: run the maths — a £6,000 car cannot carry an £8,000 engine ⚠️. P38/older: used engine or specialist rebuild as restoration, not repair. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your car.

mainEntity.name: Which Range Rover should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: Late L405 SDV8 for the sweet spot; L460 D350 if the budget is new-car sized; a sorted BMW-era L322 Td6 for the informed bargain; a Classic if your heart has already decided.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stampings vary by era. Or tell us your generation and variant (SDV6, SDV8, D350, P530) and we'll identify it.

mainEntity.name: Range Rover vs Range Rover Sport — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mechanically they're siblings — shared platforms and engines mean shared strengths and shared risks. The Sport's harder driven life shows in our enquiry patterns; the full-size car's extra weight works everything else harder. Buy on engine and history, not badge.

hero.tagPill: Range Rover • 1970–Present • 5 Generations • TDV6, TDV8, SDV8, V8, Ingenium
hero.h1: Range Rover Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every generation. Every engine code. Every known failure. Every replacement cost. From the original V8 to the L460's twin-turbo flagship — backed by 468+ real UK Range Rover enquiries [EM-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The Widest Swing in the Range — a £6,500 engine is trivial in a £60,000 L405 and terminal in a £6,000 L322. Generation decides everything.
★★★☆☆ — the engines are mostly better than the reputation; the air suspension and electronics around them earn the reputation
4.4 SDV8 (448DT) — the most dependable big diesel JLR made • BMW M57 Td6 (L322 era)
3.0 TDV6/SDV6 (306DT) — crankshaft bearing • 3.6 TDV8 (368DT) — timing chain/tensioner
Late L405 4.4 SDV8 Vogue — flagship luxury, the range's strongest engine
L460 D350 — the AJ20D6 six carries none of the old V6 legacy
👑 Flagship — and home of our Most Dependable Big Engine (4.4 SDV8)

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The Range Rover is where engine economics get emotional — the same reconditioned V8 that's a rounding error on an L460 invoice is a death sentence for a cheap L322, and knowing which side of that line your car sits on is worth more than any spec sheet.

generations.h2: Range Rover Generations — Find Your Vehicle
generations.subHeadline: Fifty-plus years, five generations, and five completely different ownership propositions.

cards.title: Range Rover Classic
cards.verdict: Now a rising classic where engine work is restoration, not repair — values increasingly justify proper rebuilds. The Tdi diesels and well-fed V8s go on forever; the bodywork is the project.

cards.title: Range Rover P38A
cards.verdict: The brave buy. V8 liner and head issues plus period electronics keep values low — which is exactly why a sorted one is the cheapest real Range Rover experience on earth. Enthusiasts only.

cards.title: Range Rover L322
cards.verdict: The £8,000 Range Rover trap and the £20,000 Range Rover bargain are the same car with different histories. BMW-era engines are the sleeper choice; budget for the air suspension either way.

cards.title: Range Rover L405
cards.verdict: The modern sweet spot — aluminium body, real depreciation absorbed, and the SDV8 at its peak. The TDV6's crank-bearing question is the one cloud: buy the V8 or buy the paperwork.

cards.title: Range Rover L460
cards.meta: 2022–present • D300/D350 (AJ20D6), P400 (AJ20P6), P440e/P510e PHEV, P530 4.4 V8 (BMW-derived)
cards.verdict: Early-build electronics gremlins made headlines; the engines themselves are proving strong. At these values, no engine bill will ever total the car — the safest replacement economics in the entire JLR range.

engineDatabase.h2: Range Rover Engine Codes — The Complete Database

engines.enquiries: 620 JLR-wide [EM-VERIFIED — recorded as "508PS" in source data; see correction note]
— (aggregated under 306DT in source data — split before citing)

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: 306DT, 368DT and 448DT ratings: 93% confidence — high-volume engines in our dataset [EM-VERIFIED] with deep specialist rebuild history. Classic, P38 and BMW-era L322 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns, no proprietary volume data. L460 engines: Provisional — the parc is too young to rate with volume data; ratings reflect early patterns and shared-architecture evidence. Data note (internal) — TWO CORRECTIONS APPLIED, verify at source: 1. "508PS" is not the 4.4 SDV8. In JLR coding, 508PS is the 5.0 supercharged petrol (AJ133 family; 508PN = naturally aspirated). The 4.4 SDV8 diesel is 448DT. The source sheets and homepage both label the SDV8 as "508PS" — the reliability claim about the SDV8 is right, the code is wrong. The 620 enquiries recorded against "508PS" almost certainly belong to the 448DT SDV8 and are shown there pending verification. Correct this across the homepage, Discovery legacy sheet and Table A before launch — it's the exact kind of error a JLR specialist would spot instantly. 2. AJ20D6 split applied (as on Defender/Discovery): late-L405 and L460 D300/D350 are the Ingenium six, not the 306DT V6.

problems.description: The L405 TDV6/SDV6's defining risk — cold-start knock is the warning, and once the bottom end lets go there's rarely anything to rebuild. £6,500–£9,500 recon [EM-QUOTE].
The 3.6 TDV8's known weakness — chain and guide wear with snap risk on neglected oil intervals. Affects L322 2006–2010.
Supercharger nose bearing whine plus timing chain guide wear on early 5.0s — noisy warning, expensive silence. Affects L322/L405 petrols.

problems.issue: P38 Rover V8 Liner & Head Issues
problems.description: Slipped cylinder liners and porous blocks on the 4.0/4.6 — the failure that halved P38 values. Top-hat liner rebuilds fix it permanently.
Every generation from Classic LSE onward; compressor burnout presents a fire risk. The most common non-engine enquiry across all Range Rovers.
Short-run D300/D350s dilute oil through interrupted DPF regens; P400e engines that never run suffer their own neglect patterns. Modern problems for modern Range Rovers.

marketIntelligence.h2: Range Rover Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising as the parc exits warranty cover [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬇ Falling with the L322 parc [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: A £150,000 flagship generating 184 engine enquiries in its first years [EM-VERIFIED] is unusual — L460 demand is dominated by extended-warranty and due-diligence questions, not failures. Owners are nervous about the headlines, not the engines. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

L405 enquiries concentrate heavily on the TDV6/SDV6 — the SDV8, despite the biggest bills, barely features in failure enquiries. The most expensive engine is the cheapest to own per mile. (Editorial synthesis.)
368DT demand is declining in step with L322 values — the same end-of-economics curve we track on the Discovery 3. (Editorial synthesis.)
marketIntelligence.liveEnquiryFeedNote: Live Enquiry Feed module embeds here.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every Range Rover Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one Range Rover based on this page, buy a late L405 4.4 SDV8 — the most dependable big engine JLR ever made, wrapped in the last of the pre-L460 depreciation. And if a £7,000 L322 is calling you: the car is £7,000, the ownership isn't.

replacementCosts.h2: Range Rover Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.figuresNote: 306DT, 368DT, 448DT, AJ133, 204PT, AJ20D6 figures [EM-QUOTE]; Classic, P38, BMW-era and L460 rows [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs Table A].
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: 10–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [EM-QUOTE]. The Range Rover rule — generation is destiny: The same £8,000–£12,000 SDV8 recon is 15% of a nice L405's value (✅ Safe), 60%+ of a tired L322's (⚠️ threshold), and more than a whole P38 (❌ walk away, or buy a used engine). Before any Range Rover engine decision, value the car first — we'll do it with you for free.

engineEvolution.h2: How the Range Rover Engine Range Evolved — 1970 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: The Buick-derived V8 defined the car; diesels arrived only when fuel prices demanded them
eras.worthKnowing: The Classic is now the only Range Rover whose engine work is an investment

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW ownership brought German engines — the M57 Td6 remains one of the most dependable diesels ever fitted to a Range Rover
eras.worthKnowing: The sleeper knowledge: early L322s are more BMW than Land Rover, and better for it

eras.whyBmwChanged: Ford brought Jaguar's V8s and the joint-venture TDV8s — huge performance gains, new failure modes
eras.worthKnowing: The 368DT's chain issue and the 448DT's near-flawless record are one lesson apart

eras.whyBmwChanged: The aluminium L405 demanded efficiency — the V6 diesel carried the volume and the crank-bearing risk with it
eras.worthKnowing: The SDV8 outlived the SDV6's reputation for a reason

eras.whyBmwChanged: Electrification retired the old diesels; for the V8, JLR went back to BMW — full circle after 25 years
eras.worthKnowing: The cleanest engine slate since the Classic, at the highest prices in the car's history

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a Range Rover?

profiles.verdict: An L405 is a magnificent family flagship — budget running costs like a second mortgage payment and it will never disappoint.
The wrong tool. Short runs punish the diesels' DPFs and the PHEVs only make sense with a driveway charger and discipline.
3,500kg with imperious ease — the SDV8 L405 may be the finest tow car ever sold in Britain.
The most dangerous badge on this site for a tight budget. A cheap L322 or P38 is not a cheap Range Rover — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody.
Classics are appreciating assets worth proper engine investment; a cherished SDV8 L405 is a future classic being written now.
